_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Merlini et al. 2011 NEJM (PMID 21506742) and Ruberg et al. 2019 Circ (PMID 30865482). AL amyloidosis treatment per Kumar et al. Leukemia 2012 PMID 22441209. Tafamidis trial Maurer et al. NEJM 2018 (PMID 30145929). ATTR epidemiology from Buxbaum et al. Circ HF 2010. Diagnostic criteria from ISAD/BSH guidelines 2020.

h1Title: Amyloidosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Amyloidosis is a group of disorders in which abnormally folded proteins aggregate into insoluble amyloid fibrils that deposit in organs and tissues, disrupting their architecture and function. Over 30 precursor proteins can form amyloid; the most clinically important types are AL amyloidosis (from immunoglobulin light chains in plasma cell dyscrasias), ATTR amyloidosis (from misfolded transthyretin, either hereditary or wild-type), and AA amyloidosis (from serum amyloid A protein in chronic inflammatory disease). AL amyloidosis affects approximately 10-15 per million people per year in the United States and United Kingdom; wild-type ATTR cardiac amyloidosis is increasingly recognized as an underdiagnosed cause of heart failure in patients over 65. Effective disease-modifying therapies now exist for both AL and ATTR amyloidosis, transforming previously fatal diseases into manageable chronic conditions.

keyStats.prevalence: AL amyloidosis: 10-15 per million per year in Western populations; ATTR amyloidosis: estimated 0.5-1% of patients over 75 with heart failure; AA amyloidosis prevalence tied to underlying inflammatory disease burden
keyStats.demographics: AL amyloidosis predominantly affects adults over 50; hereditary ATTR (hATTR) affects all ages but typically presents in fourth to sixth decade; wild-type ATTR (wtATTR) predominantly affects men over 65
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at AL amyloidosis diagnosis approximately 63 years; wild-type ATTR presents typically at age 75-80
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 3,000-5,000 new AL amyloidosis diagnoses per year in the UK; prevalence of ATTR cardiac amyloidosis estimated at 250,000-500,000 in the US based on autopsy and cardiac MRI studies

definition: Amyloidosis (ICD-10: E85.9) refers to a spectrum of diseases defined by the extracellular deposition of insoluble protein fibrils with a characteristic beta-sheet secondary structure, visualized as apple-green birefringence on Congo red-stained tissue sections under polarized light. All amyloid deposits share the common fibril structure, but the precursor protein varies by disease subtype. In AL amyloidosis — the most common systemic type in developed countries — monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (most often lambda) produced by a plasma cell clone misfold and aggregate in the heart, kidneys, liver, peripheral nerves, and soft tissues. In ATTR amyloidosis, the transthyretin (TTR) protein — a hepatic carrier of thyroid hormone and retinol — dissociates from its native tetramer and refolds into fibrils that deposit predominantly in the heart and peripheral nervous system; hereditary forms result from over 120 known point mutations in the TTR gene, with the Val122Ile variant prevalent in 3.4% of African Americans. AA amyloidosis, the leading type in developing countries, arises from serum amyloid A protein produced in chronic inflammatory states. Specialists from multiple disciplines — hematology, cardiology, nephrology, and neurology — manage amyloidosis according to the organ systems involved.

typesClassification.description: The most common systemic amyloidosis in developed countries. Caused by a monoclonal plasma cell clone — usually not a frank myeloma — that secretes misfolding light chains (predominantly lambda in 75% of cases). Deposits in heart, kidneys, liver, peripheral nerves, and soft tissues. Median survival without treatment 12-18 months from cardiac diagnosis.

Caused by one of over 120 point mutations in the TTR gene encoding transthyretin. Over 150,000 people worldwide are estimated to carry TTR mutations. Val30Met (prevalent in Portuguese, Swedish, and Japanese families) causes predominantly peripheral neuropathy; Val122Ile (present in 3.4% of African Americans) causes cardiomyopathy.

typesClassification.type: Wild-type ATTR amyloidosis (wtATTR, senile cardiac amyloidosis)
typesClassification.description: Normal (unmutated) TTR protein deposits in cardiac tissue in older adults without a TTR mutation. Predominantly affects men over 65; found in up to 25% of hearts of octogenarians at autopsy. Presents as heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome preceding cardiac symptoms by years.

Complicates chronic inflammatory disorders — rheumatoid arthritis (the most common cause in Western countries), ankylosing spondylitis, IBD, chronic infection (tuberculosis, bronchiectasis), and periodic fever syndromes. Deposits predominantly in kidneys, liver, and spleen. Prevalence falls with effective treatment of the underlying inflammatory disease.

Include apolipoprotein A-I amyloidosis (liver, heart, kidney), fibrinogen alpha-chain amyloidosis (renal dominant), and lysozyme amyloidosis. All are autosomal-dominant and diagnosed by genetic testing when systemic amyloidosis is found without a plasma cell clone or TTR mutation.

primarySymptoms: Progressive dyspnea on exertion and ankle edema from restrictive cardiomyopathy, caused by amyloid fibril infiltration stiffening the myocardium — the presenting symptom in over 70% of AL and ATTR cardiac amyloidosis.
Nephrotic-range proteinuria (protein excretion above 3.5 g/24 h) with frothy urine and dependent edema in AL or AA amyloid nephropathy from glomerular fibril deposition.
Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome — often preceding cardiac symptoms by 5-10 years in wild-type ATTR — causing nocturnal hand pain, numbness, and weakness in the median nerve distribution.

Peripheral and autonomic neuropathy in hATTR — length-dependent sensory loss in feet and hands, orthostatic hypotension, erectile dysfunction, bladder dysfunction, and diarrhea alternating with constipation.
Macroglossia (enlarged tongue) with serrated lateral borders from soft-tissue amyloid deposition — a pathognomonic finding specific to AL amyloidosis, seen in approximately 15% of cases.
Periorbital purpura ('raccoon eyes' appearance) from amyloid-fragile dermal capillaries rupturing after minor trauma, straining, or Valsalva maneuver — found in 15-20% of AL amyloidosis.
Hepatomegaly with a firm, non-tender liver that may be massive (liver span above 15 cm) but usually with preserved liver function tests until very late in the disease.
Orthostatic hypotension without compensatory tachycardia from autonomic neuropathy, particularly in hATTR Val30Met, causing recurrent syncope and falls.
Unexplained bilateral symmetric sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy in the lower limbs, often with early small-fiber involvement causing burning pain or temperature insensitivity.
Splenomegaly with hyposplenism from splenic amyloid deposition, increasing susceptibility to encapsulated bacterial infections in long-standing AA or AL amyloidosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome in a man over 60, especially if not associated with hypothyroidism or diabetes — a 5-10 year prodrome of wild-type ATTR cardiac amyloidosis in a substantial minority of patients.
Unexplained light-chain M-protein or plasma cell dyscrasia on serum protein electrophoresis or free light chain assay in a patient with unexplained proteinuria or heart failure.

Echocardiographic finding of left ventricular hypertrophy with a granular sparkling texture and preserved or reduced ejection fraction — 'thickened walls' without hypertension raises amyloid as a differential.
Orthostatic hypotension out of proportion to the degree of heart failure, or worsening with diuretics, in a patient with heart failure — suggests concomitant autonomic neuropathy from amyloid.
Diffuse peripheral neuropathy with concurrent nephrotic syndrome in a patient without diabetes — the combination should trigger screening for systemic amyloidosis.
emergencySigns: Acute decompensated heart failure with low cardiac output, hypotension, and renal impairment in AL cardiac amyloidosis — standard heart failure drugs (digoxin, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers) are dangerous and contraindicated in cardiac amyloidosis.

Sudden cardiac death or malignant arrhythmia in cardiac amyloidosis — amyloid infiltration disrupts the conduction system, producing complete heart block and ventricular arrhythmias without warning in some patients.
Acute kidney injury with oliguric nephrotic syndrome and rapidly rising creatinine — amyloid cast nephropathy or amyloid-related vascular thrombosis requires urgent hematology-nephrology evaluation.
Spontaneous splenic rupture in massive splenic amyloidosis — rare but life-threatening hemorrhage requiring emergency splenectomy.
Severe bleeding from acquired coagulation factor X deficiency, which is selectively adsorbed by amyloid fibrils, causing unexpected bleeding complications during procedures.

causes.cause: Plasma cell dyscrasia producing misfolding immunoglobulin light chains (AL amyloidosis)
causes.description: A monoclonal plasma cell clone — usually comprising less than 10% of bone marrow plasma cells — produces immunoglobulin light chains (predominantly lambda) with an intrinsic tendency to misfold and aggregate into amyloid fibrils. The clone is rarely a frank multiple myeloma; most patients have MGUS-level disease at diagnosis. Treatment targets the plasma cell clone.

causes.cause: TTR gene mutation causing destabilized transthyretin tetramer (hATTR amyloidosis)
causes.description: Over 120 mutations in the TTR gene cause the tetrameric transthyretin protein to dissociate prematurely into monomers that misfold and aggregate into fibrils. Mutations are autosomal-dominant with incomplete penetrance. The liver produces virtually all circulating TTR, explaining why liver transplantation (now largely replaced by RNA-interference therapy) originally halted progression.

causes.cause: Age-related destabilization of wild-type TTR (wtATTR amyloidosis)
causes.description: Normal, unmutated TTR becomes increasingly prone to tetramer dissociation and misfolding with advancing age, particularly in men. Wild-type TTR progressively deposits in the heart, causing restrictive cardiomyopathy. Why men are disproportionately affected and why the process accelerates after age 65 is not fully understood.

causes.cause: Chronic inflammatory disease driving sustained serum amyloid A overproduction (AA amyloidosis)
causes.description: SAA is an acute-phase protein produced by the liver during inflammation. Sustained SAA elevation above 10 mg/L for years eventually exceeds the capacity for its degradation, and fibrillar deposits accumulate in kidneys, liver, spleen, and gut. Diseases most commonly responsible include rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn disease, ankylosing spondylitis, bronchiectasis, and tuberculosis.

Beta-2 microglobulin (B2M) accumulates in patients on long-term dialysis (typically after 5-10 years) because conventional dialysis membranes do not effectively clear this protein. B2M amyloid deposits in musculoskeletal tissues causing destructive arthropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, and cystic bone lesions. High-flux dialysis membranes and hemodiafiltration have substantially reduced but not eliminated this complication.

riskFactors.description: The plasma cell clone responsible for AL amyloidosis typically originates from a pre-existing MGUS. AL amyloidosis develops in approximately 0.7-1% of MGUS patients per year; the risk is highest with elevated serum free light chains and lambda predominance.

Over 120 TTR mutations predispose to hATTR amyloidosis. Val122Ile is present in 3.4% of African Americans and causes late-onset cardiomyopathy. Val30Met is the most common mutation globally, prevalent in Portugal, Sweden, and Japan, and predominantly causes neuropathy.

Wild-type ATTR amyloidosis is overwhelmingly a disease of men over 65; prevalence approaches 25% of octogenarians at autopsy. Female sex hormones may stabilize the TTR tetramer, partially explaining the marked male predominance.

Sustained serum amyloid A above 10 mg/L for more than 10 years dramatically increases AA amyloidosis risk. Risk is substantially reduced when inflammatory disease is treated to achieve SAA levels below 10 mg/L. The transition from rheumatoid arthritis to AA amyloidosis now takes 15-20+ years with modern biologic therapy.
Beta-2 microglobulin amyloidosis risk rises with duration of dialysis; almost universal after 15 years on conventional membranes. High-flux dialysis membranes reduce but do not eliminate risk.

riskFactors.factor: Cardiac risk factors masking amyloid cardiomyopathy
riskFactors.description: Hypertensive LVH and ATTR cardiac amyloidosis can co-exist. Failure to consider amyloidosis when LVH is disproportionate to hypertension severity, or when carpal tunnel syndrome precedes heart failure in elderly men, leads to delayed diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: AL amyloidosis predominantly affects adults aged 50-70, with a slight male predominance. African Americans have a higher risk of late-onset cardiac-predominant disease from the Val122Ile TTR variant affecting 3.4% of this population.
Wild-type ATTR cardiac amyloidosis almost exclusively affects men over 65; the diagnosis is frequently missed in patients presenting as heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.

hATTR amyloidosis has geographic clustering: Portugal (Val30Met neuropathy), Sweden (Val30Met with cardiomyopathy), Japan (Val30Met), and African-descended populations globally (Val122Ile cardiomyopathy).

AA amyloidosis prevalence correlates with inflammatory disease burden; it is more common in developing countries where tuberculosis and chronic infections sustain high SAA levels, and in populations with higher rates of untreated inflammatory arthritis.
diagnosisOverview: Amyloidosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy demonstrating Congo red-positive deposits with apple-green birefringence under polarized light. Preferred biopsy sites offering the highest diagnostic yield with least morbidity are abdominal fat aspirate (positive in 60-80% of AL and 45-65% of ATTR amyloidosis) and minor salivary gland biopsy. Rectum, kidney, liver, cardiac, and nerve biopsies are used when fat aspirate is negative and clinical suspicion remains high. Once amyloid is confirmed histologically, typing the fibril protein is essential to direct treatment — this is done by laser microdissection proteomics, immunohistochemistry, or immunoelectron microscopy. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation, serum free light chains (sFLC), and bone marrow biopsy establish the plasma cell clone in AL amyloidosis. For ATTR amyloidosis, 99mTc-DPD/PYP scintigraphy (bone scintigraphy) has 91-100% sensitivity and 89-97% specificity for cardiac ATTR amyloidosis in the absence of a monoclonal protein, effectively becoming a non-invasive cardiac diagnosis tool. TTR gene sequencing distinguishes hATTR from wtATTR. For AA amyloidosis, serum amyloid A levels and the underlying inflammatory disease history guide the workup. Cardiac biomarkers (NT-proBNP, troponin T) and echocardiography with advanced deformation imaging stage AL cardiac disease using the Mayo 2004/2012 staging systems, which strongly predict median survival.

diagnosticTests.test: Abdominal fat aspirate with Congo red staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Least invasive biopsy yielding a diagnosis of systemic amyloidosis in 60-80% of AL cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic injection in the periumbilical abdomen; a needle aspirates a small amount of subcutaneous fat. Minor procedure, outpatient. Results in 3-5 days. Apple-green birefringence under polarized light confirms amyloid. Negative result does not exclude amyloidosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum free light chains (sFLC) and serum/urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the monoclonal plasma cell clone responsible for AL amyloidosis; negative in ATTR and AA amyloidosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood and urine samples. Results in 2-5 days. An abnormal free light chain ratio (above 3 or below 0.26 kappa:lambda) with an absolute free light chain excess strongly suggests a plasma cell clone. Immunofixation identifies the monoclonal protein isotype.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive diagnosis of ATTR cardiac amyloidosis when grade 2-3 cardiac uptake is combined with absent monoclonal protein
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiotracer is injected IV; imaging at 3 hours. A 30-minute scan lying still. Grade 2-3 cardiac uptake equal to or greater than bone uptake, combined with a negative serum/urine monoclonal protein screen, has near 100% positive predictive value for cardiac ATTR amyloidosis — cardiac biopsy can often be avoided.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects amyloid cardiomyopathy, quantifies diastolic and systolic dysfunction, and identifies the characteristic apical-sparing pattern of GLS seen in amyloid
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard 30-minute ultrasound. Granular sparkling texture, concentric thickening, diastolic dysfunction, and apical-sparing GLS (strain preserved at apex relative to basal segments) are strongly suggestive. Pericardial effusion and biatrial enlargement may be present.

diagnosticTests.test: Cardiac MRI with gadolinium (late gadolinium enhancement)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates diffuse subendocardial and transmural late gadolinium enhancement and T1-mapping abnormalities characteristic of amyloid infiltration, and quantifies myocardial mass
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scanner; gadolinium contrast injected IV. Diffuse subendocardial LGE is highly sensitive and specific for cardiac amyloid. Not required when scintigraphy is diagnostic but adds prognostic data.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes hereditary ATTR (one of 120+ mutations) from wild-type ATTR amyloidosis, informing prognosis, treatment choice, and family screening
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample sent to molecular genetics. Results in 2-4 weeks. All patients with confirmed ATTR amyloidosis should have TTR sequencing. Positive result mandates screening of first-degree relatives.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of amyloidosis requires two parallel strategies: suppressing fibril precursor production and managing organ damage. For AL amyloidosis, the plasma cell clone is treated with plasma-cell-directed chemotherapy. The current standard of care for transplant-eligible patients is bortezomib-based induction (VCd — bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone) followed by autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) in selected patients. Non-transplant-eligible patients receive bortezomib-based regimens (VCd or VD) or the newer daratumumab-based combinations — daratumumab plus VCd has shown superior response rates in the ANDROMEDA trial (2021), achieving hematological complete response in 53% versus 18% with VCd alone. Hematologic response — monitored by sFLC — must be achieved rapidly because cardiac damage is progressive; a very good partial response (VGPR) or better reduces mortality significantly. For ATTR amyloidosis, tafamidis (a TTR-stabilizer, 80 mg daily) demonstrated a 30% reduction in all-cause mortality and a 32% reduction in hospitalization over 30 months in the ATTR-ACT trial (Maurer et al. 2018, NEJM), and is FDA-approved for ATTR cardiomyopathy. Patisiran (RNA interference) and inotersen (antisense oligonucleotide) reduce hepatic TTR production by 80% and are approved for hATTR polyneuropathy; they also improve cardiac outcomes. Vutrisiran (subcutaneous RNAi, quarterly) is approved for hATTR polyneuropathy and has cardiac outcome data. For AA amyloidosis, aggressive treatment of the underlying inflammatory disease with biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, IL-1 inhibitors for periodic fevers) reduces SAA and can stabilize or improve renal function. Supportive organ-specific management is essential throughout — diuretics for congestion, ACE inhibitors for renal protection in nephrotic-range proteinuria, elastic stockings and midodrine for orthostatic hypotension, and renal replacement therapy when GFR falls below 15 mL/min.

medicalTreatments.name: Daratumumab + bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone (Dara-VCd)
medicalTreatments.description: Current preferred front-line regimen for AL amyloidosis based on the ANDROMEDA phase III trial. Daratumumab (anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody) combined with bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone achieves deep hematological responses. Given as subcutaneous daratumumab injections on a set schedule with oral and IV chemotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hematological complete response 53% vs 18% with VCd alone at 6 months; major organ response 54% vs 27% at 6 months (Kastritis et al. 2021, ANDROMEDA)

medicalTreatments.name: Bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone (VCd)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard triplet chemotherapy for AL amyloidosis; used when daratumumab is unavailable or as a non-daratumumab option. Bortezomib inhibits the 26S proteasome, triggering plasma cell apoptosis; given by subcutaneous injection twice weekly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hematological complete response in 30-35% of patients; overall response rate 60-70%

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) for AL amyloidosis
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose melphalan conditioning followed by infusion of autologous collected stem cells. Eligible patients (younger, good organ function, less cardiac involvement) achieve the deepest and most durable hematological responses. Preceded by 2-4 cycles of VCd or Dara-VCd induction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hematological complete response in 40-50% of ASCT patients; median overall survival above 8-10 years in complete responders in selected series

medicalTreatments.name: Tafamidis 80 mg oral daily (ATTR cardiomyopathy)
medicalTreatments.description: First-in-class TTR tetramer stabilizer that prevents TTR monomer dissociation and subsequent fibril formation. FDA-approved for wtATTR and hATTR cardiomyopathy based on ATTR-ACT trial. Must be started before advanced cardiac disease; less effective in NYHA class III-IV.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30% relative reduction in all-cause mortality and 32% reduction in cardiovascular hospitalization over 30 months vs placebo in ATTR-ACT (Maurer et al. NEJM 2018)

medicalTreatments.name: Patisiran or vutrisiran (RNA interference therapy for hATTR neuropathy)
medicalTreatments.description: Small interfering RNA (siRNA) formulations delivered IV (patisiran) or SC quarterly (vutrisiran) that silence TTR mRNA in hepatocytes, reducing circulating TTR by approximately 80%. Approved for hATTR polyneuropathy; vutrisiran has additional cardiac outcome data in the HELIOS-B trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modified Neuropathy Impairment Score improved in 51% of patisiran patients vs 10% with placebo in APOLLO trial; vutrisiran non-inferior to patisiran for neuropathy with improved cardiac biomarkers

medicalTreatments.name: Biologic therapies (TNF-inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, IL-1 inhibitors) for AA amyloidosis
medicalTreatments.description: Targeting the underlying inflammatory disease to reduce serum amyloid A to below 10 mg/L halts AA fibril deposition and can lead to organ regression over years. IL-6 inhibitor tocilizumab is particularly effective for inflammatory arthritis-related AA amyloidosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieving SAA below 10 mg/L prevents progression in greater than 85% of AA amyloidosis patients and allows partial renal recovery in approximately 30-40% in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation (hATTR amyloidosis, now largely replaced)
surgicalOptions.description: Historically the primary therapy for hATTR; the liver produces nearly all circulating TTR, so transplantation halts amyloid formation from the mutant TTR protein. Now largely supplanted by RNA interference therapy for neuropathy, but still used in selected younger patients with early hATTR neuropathy and limited cardiac involvement.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological stabilization in 50-70% of Val30Met patients with early-stage neuropathy; less effective for cardiomyopathy; cardiac deposits from wild-type TTR can continue after liver transplantation

surgicalOptions.name: Heart transplantation (AL amyloidosis with end-stage cardiac disease)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed rarely and only in patients with AL amyloidosis who have achieved hematological complete response (no active plasma cell clone) — otherwise amyloid re-deposits in the donor heart. Five-year survival is approximately 50-60% in highly selected cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Post-transplant survival comparable to other cardiac transplant indications only when the underlying plasma cell clone is eradicated first

surgicalOptions.name: Renal transplantation (AA or AL amyloidosis with end-stage renal disease)
surgicalOptions.description: Renal transplantation for end-stage renal disease from amyloid nephropathy is feasible when the underlying disease (plasma cell clone or inflammation) is controlled. Without amyloid suppression, amyloid re-deposits in the transplanted kidney within 2-5 years.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival 5 years post-transplant approximately 70-80% when underlying amyloid disease is suppressed; significantly lower with active disease

alternativeTreatments.name: CPHPC (bi-functional compound for SAP depletion) — investigational
alternativeTreatments.description: R-1-[6-[R-2-carboxy-pyrrolidin-1-yl]-6-oxo-hexanoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carboxylic acid (CPHPC) combined with anti-SAP antibodies is under clinical investigation; removes serum amyloid P component that stabilizes amyloid deposits. Early-phase trials show fibril depletion in liver and other organs.

whySeeSpecialist: Amyloidosis requires specialist management by a multidisciplinary team — hematology for AL (plasma cell clone management), cardiology for cardiac amyloidosis, nephrology for renal involvement, and neurology for neuropathy. Centers with dedicated amyloid programs have substantially better outcomes because of access to ASCT, RNAi therapies, and diagnostic expertise in subtyping.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a center with a dedicated systemic amyloidosis program offering the full diagnostic pipeline (fat pad aspiration, proteomics, scintigraphy), treatment access (ASCT, daratumumab combinations, tafamidis, RNAi therapy), and the multidisciplinary team needed for complex organ management. In the UK, the National Amyloidosis Centre (UCL/Royal Free Hospital) and equivalent centers are the standard of care.
hospitalCriteria: Access to 99mTc-DPD or PYP scintigraphy for non-invasive ATTR cardiac diagnosis
Hematology unit with ASCT capability and daratumumab access for AL amyloidosis
Specialist cardiology with experience in amyloid cardiomyopathy management
Nephrology unit for renal amyloidosis management and renal replacement therapy
Molecular genetics for TTR gene sequencing and amyloid proteomics laboratory
keyFacilities: Dedicated systemic amyloidosis centers with multidisciplinary programs
Stem cell transplant units with AL amyloidosis ASCT experience
Advanced cardiac imaging services (echocardiography with GLS, cardiac MRI)
Nuclear medicine departments with cardiac amyloid scintigraphy programs
preventionStrategies: Screen first-degree relatives of patients with confirmed hereditary ATTR amyloidosis for the family-specific TTR mutation — early detection enables treatment before irreversible organ damage.
Treat underlying inflammatory diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, bronchiectasis, chronic infections) to sustained remission to prevent AA amyloidosis; target serum amyloid A below 10 mg/L.

Monitor serum free light chains annually in patients with MGUS — a rising involved free light chain or change in ratio should prompt re-staging to detect AL amyloidosis at an early, more treatable stage.
Use high-flux dialysis membranes and hemodiafiltration in patients on long-term renal replacement therapy to minimize beta-2 microglobulin accumulation.
Consider periodic echocardiography and serum sFLC in African American patients over 60 carrying Val122Ile, in whom treatment with tafamidis can now slow cardiac progression.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid fluid retention by adhering to prescribed diuretic regimens; amyloid cardiomyopathy is very sensitive to volume overload, and small changes in fluid status cause disproportionate symptoms.

Avoid digoxin, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem), and high-dose ACE inhibitors in cardiac amyloidosis — amyloid fibrils bind digoxin with high affinity causing toxicity at normal doses, and these drugs cause profound hypotension.
Wear elastic compression stockings and take midodrine as prescribed for orthostatic hypotension; avoid abrupt position changes and prolonged standing.
Limit dietary sodium (below 2 g/day) and maintain fluid restriction as directed by the clinical team when nephrotic syndrome or cardiac congestion is present.

Attend all scheduled hematological response and organ function monitoring — response rates to chemotherapy in AL amyloidosis determine survival, and early treatment adjustment based on sFLC is critical.
Report any new bleeding, bruising, or unusual swelling promptly — acquired factor X deficiency and soft-tissue amyloid can cause unexpected hemorrhage.

recommended: Moderate sodium intake (below 2 g/day) when heart failure or nephrotic syndrome is present, in coordination with the care team
Adequate protein intake despite proteinuria — work with a renal dietitian to balance protein needs against worsening albuminuria
Small frequent meals if gastroparesis or GI dysmotility from autonomic neuropathy causes early satiety
Adequate caloric intake to maintain body weight during chemotherapy, when anorexia and GI side effects reduce intake
avoid: Licorice root supplements containing glycyrrhizin, which may worsen fluid retention
High-sodium convenience foods and processed meats that exacerbate fluid accumulation in heart failure or nephrotic syndrome
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), which worsen renal function in amyloid nephropathy and can precipitate acute kidney injury
Grapefruit and pomelo with tafamidis — consult prescribing guidelines as tafamidis metabolism may be affected

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity should be guided by functional capacity and organ involvement. Mild-to-moderate exercise (walking, yoga, swimming at low intensity) is beneficial for functional capacity and wellbeing in patients with well-compensated cardiac and renal amyloidosis. High-intensity exercise and contact sports are contraindicated with significant cardiac amyloidosis — the risk of arrhythmia and decompensation from exertion is elevated. Patients with autonomic neuropathy and orthostatic hypotension should exercise seated or recumbent when possible and avoid rapid position changes.
dailyManagement: Take tafamidis, patisiran, or vutrisiran as prescribed without interruption — stopping TTR-targeted therapy allows TTR fibril deposition to resume.
Weigh daily and report a gain above 2 kg within 48 hours to the care team — rapid weight gain indicates fluid retention from cardiac decompensation.
Monitor serum free light chains regularly during AL treatment (monthly initially) — rising sFLC predicts progression before clinical deterioration.
Keep a medications list highlighting contraindicated drugs (digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem) so emergency physicians can avoid administering them.
Use compression garments and follow orthostatic hypotension precautions daily; have midodrine prescribed for standing-related syncope.
Attend monthly or quarterly multidisciplinary reviews, alternating between hematology, cardiology, nephrology, and neurology as the organ burden dictates.
prognosis: Prognosis in amyloidosis is highly subtype-specific and has improved dramatically over the past decade. AL amyloidosis without treatment had a median survival of 12-18 months from cardiac diagnosis; with modern daratumumab-based chemotherapy, patients achieving hematological complete response have median survival exceeding 8-10 years in registry data. The Mayo cardiac staging system (stages I-IV based on NT-proBNP, troponin T, and sFLC) stratifies median survival from over 5 years (stage I) to under 5 months (stage IV) without treatment, making rapid hematological response the primary treatment goal. ATTR cardiomyopathy treated with tafamidis shows a 30% reduction in mortality at 30 months versus placebo; survival beyond 5 years is increasingly observed in treated patients. hATTR neuropathy treated with patisiran or inotersen stabilizes or improves neurological function in the majority. AA amyloidosis can partially reverse with effective control of the underlying inflammatory disease — improvement in renal function occurs in approximately 30-40% of patients who achieve SAA below 10 mg/L. Predictors of poor prognosis in AL amyloidosis include advanced cardiac stage, low blood pressure, renal failure requiring dialysis, and elevated troponin — all reflecting the extent of organ infiltration at diagnosis.
recoveryTimeline: In AL amyloidosis, hematological response to Dara-VCd is typically evident within 3-4 cycles (approximately 3 months); organ responses (falling NT-proBNP, improving renal function) lag by 3-12 months. ASCT patients return to normal activity in 3-6 months. Tafamidis begins reducing ATTR fibril deposition immediately, but echocardiographic and clinical improvement is gradual over 2-3 years. RNAi therapy for hATTR neuropathy shows first neurological stabilization at 6-9 months with continued improvement over 18-24 months. AA amyloidosis organ recovery occurs slowly over 1-3 years of sustained SAA suppression.
complications: Cardiac arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death from amyloid infiltration of the conduction system and myocardium — the leading cause of death in AL and ATTR cardiac amyloidosis.
End-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation from glomerular amyloid deposition in AL and AA amyloidosis — occurs in approximately 20-30% of patients in some series.

Acquired factor X deficiency from selective fibril-adsorption of clotting factor, causing unexpected surgical or spontaneous bleeding — diagnosed by coagulation screen and treated with fresh frozen plasma and chemotherapy.

Treatment-related toxicities from chemotherapy (AL) — peripheral neuropathy from bortezomib, fluid retention from dexamethasone, cytopenias, and treatment-related mortality during ASCT of approximately 2-5% in experienced centers.
Progressive autonomic neuropathy in hATTR causing GI dysmotility (diarrhea, constipation, gastroparesis), recurrent syncope, and urinary retention when treatment is delayed.

Opportunistic infection from immunosuppression by plasma cell dyscrasia and chemotherapy in AL amyloidosis — prophylactic antiviral (valacyclovir) and Pneumocystis prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) are standard during bortezomib-based therapy.

supportResources.name: Amyloidosis Research Consortium
supportResources.description: US non-profit research and patient advocacy organization funding amyloidosis clinical trials and providing patient education resources on all amyloidosis types.

supportResources.name: National Amyloidosis Centre (UCL/Royal Free Hospital, UK)
supportResources.description: The UK national reference center for all types of systemic amyloidosis, providing diagnosis, treatment, and research for patients from across the UK and internationally.

supportResources.name: Myeloma UK — AL Amyloidosis Resources
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing accessible patient guides on AL amyloidosis, treatment pathways, and clinical trials information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cardiac amyloidosis presents as heart failure but with key distinguishing features: LV wall thickness disproportionate to blood pressure, low-voltage ECG despite thick walls, apical-sparing strain on echo, and worsening with digoxin. Standard heart failure drugs are often harmful in amyloid cardiomyopathy. 99mTc-DPD scintigraphy and fat pad biopsy differentiate amyloid from standard heart failure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AL amyloidosis arises from a plasma cell clone (not a lymphoma), though both can cause constitutional symptoms, organomegaly, and abnormal protein in the blood. Lymphoma features lymph node enlargement, CD20-positive B-cells on biopsy, and elevated LDH; AL amyloidosis is diagnosed by Congo red-positive tissue deposits and a plasma cell dyscrasia on marrow and protein studies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amyloid nephropathy presents as nephrotic syndrome (massive proteinuria, edema) in a patient whose blood pressure and GFR may be surprisingly well-preserved initially. CKD from other causes rarely causes nephrotic-range proteinuria without glomerulonephritis. Renal biopsy with Congo red stain is definitive.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve hormone-related pathology managed by endocrinology; pituitary adenomas can co-occur in MEN1 syndrome which also encompasses neuroendocrine tumors that may occasionally cause localized amyloid deposits.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: ATTR amyloidosis affects transthyretin, a thyroid hormone carrier protein; thyroid function tests are used in the differential workup of amyloidosis when TTR variants are suspected.

relatedConditions.name: Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Anemia of chronic disease is common in AL amyloidosis due to the underlying plasma cell dyscrasia and kidney involvement; monitoring and management of anemia is part of routine amyloidosis care.

relatedConditions.name: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are rare single-gene protein-folding disorders managed at specialist centers, sharing diagnostic workup principles of protein phenotyping and genetic testing.

faqs.question: What is amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: Amyloidosis is a group of diseases in which abnormally folded proteins deposit as insoluble fibrils in organs, disrupting their function. Over 30 proteins can form amyloid. The most common types are AL amyloidosis (from plasma cell immunoglobulin light chains) and ATTR amyloidosis (from misfolded transthyretin). Organs most often affected include the heart, kidneys, nerves, and liver.

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms vary by type. Common early features include unexplained fatigue, exertional dyspnea, ankle swelling, bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, frothy urine from protein loss, and orthostatic dizziness. In AL amyloidosis, periorbital purpura and macroglossia are distinctive signs. Carpal tunnel syndrome may precede cardiac amyloidosis by years.

faqs.question: How is amyloidosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Amyloidosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy (usually abdominal fat aspirate) showing Congo red-positive deposits with apple-green birefringence. Typing the fibril requires proteomics or immunohistochemistry. For ATTR cardiac amyloidosis, 99mTc-DPD bone scintigraphy combined with negative monoclonal protein testing provides a near-definitive non-invasive diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is amyloidosis curable?
faqs.answer: AL amyloidosis can achieve long-term remission with chemotherapy and ASCT when the plasma cell clone is eliminated. ATTR amyloidosis is not curable but can be stabilized by tafamidis or RNAi therapy. AA amyloidosis can regress when the underlying inflammatory disease is controlled. Early treatment substantially improves organ outcomes and survival in all types.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AL and ATTR amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: AL amyloidosis is caused by immunoglobulin light chains from a plasma cell clone, treated with chemotherapy. ATTR amyloidosis is caused by misfolded transthyretin — either from a gene mutation (hATTR) or age-related protein instability (wild-type). ATTR is treated with tafamidis or RNA-interference therapy rather than chemotherapy.

faqs.question: Can amyloidosis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cardiac amyloidosis — from either AL or ATTR deposits — is the leading cause of death in AL amyloidosis and is increasingly recognized in elderly men with heart failure. It causes restrictive cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, and sudden death. Digoxin and calcium channel blockers are dangerous in cardiac amyloidosis.

faqs.question: What is ATTR amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: ATTR amyloidosis is caused by deposits of misfolded transthyretin (TTR) protein in the heart, nerves, and other tissues. Hereditary ATTR (hATTR) results from TTR gene mutations; wild-type ATTR occurs in older men without a mutation. Tafamidis stabilizes TTR and reduces mortality in ATTR cardiomyopathy by 30% over 30 months.

faqs.question: Is amyloidosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: ATTR amyloidosis can be hereditary (hATTR) when caused by one of 120+ autosomal-dominant TTR mutations. AL amyloidosis is not inherited. AA amyloidosis is triggered by environmental factors (chronic inflammation), not genetics. Families with confirmed hATTR should have first-degree relatives screened for the family-specific TTR mutation.

faqs.question: How is cardiac amyloidosis treated?
faqs.answer: ATTR cardiac amyloidosis is treated with tafamidis 80 mg daily, which reduces mortality and hospitalization. AL cardiac amyloidosis requires plasma cell chemotherapy (daratumumab-based regimens) targeting the underlying clone. Standard heart failure drugs (digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem) are contraindicated. Diuretics are used cautiously for congestion.

faqs.question: What is wild-type ATTR amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: Wild-type ATTR (also called senile cardiac amyloidosis) occurs when normal, unmutated transthyretin becomes unstable with age and deposits in cardiac tissue. It affects predominantly men over 65 and is a common underdiagnosed cause of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Tafamidis is FDA-approved for this form.

faqs.question: What does amyloidosis do to the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Amyloid deposits in the kidney glomeruli, causing nephrotic syndrome with protein excretion above 3.5 g/24 h, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and progressive loss of kidney function. Without treatment, AL and AA amyloidosis frequently progress to end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation.

faqs.question: What causes AL amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: AL amyloidosis is caused by a monoclonal plasma cell clone (usually not a frank myeloma) that produces immunoglobulin light chains — predominantly lambda type — with an intrinsic tendency to misfold into amyloid fibrils. Treatment targets the plasma cell clone with chemotherapy, often bortezomib and daratumumab-based regimens.

faqs.question: What is tafamidis and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Tafamidis is an oral tablet (80 mg daily) that stabilizes the transthyretin tetramer, preventing it from dissociating into monomers that misfold into amyloid fibrils. It does not dissolve existing deposits but stops new ones forming. The ATTR-ACT trial showed a 30% reduction in all-cause mortality over 30 months in ATTR cardiomyopathy.

faqs.question: Can amyloidosis cause nerve damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Peripheral neuropathy is a major feature of hATTR amyloidosis (especially Val30Met variant) and of AL amyloidosis. Symptoms include length-dependent sensory loss in feet and hands, burning pain, and autonomic dysfunction causing orthostatic hypotension, bowel and bladder problems, and erectile dysfunction.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: Prognosis depends strongly on subtype and stage. Untreated AL cardiac amyloidosis has a median survival of 12-18 months; patients achieving complete hematological response on modern therapy can survive 8-10 years. ATTR cardiomyopathy treated with tafamidis shows 30% mortality reduction at 30 months. AA amyloidosis survival depends on controlling the underlying disease.

symptomKeywords: heart failure thick LV walls elderly man
metaDescription: Amyloidosis: misfolded protein deposits in heart, kidneys and nerves. AL, ATTR and AA types, diagnosis, tafamidis and chemotherapy treatments reviewed.
